title: Meet The Parents

jokes.text: I think surprise parties are tacky, and far too often organized by well-intentioned people with very bad taste. But Patrick has always wanted one, poor thing. So I am throwing him a surprise party tonight.
jokes.setup: David declares surprise parties tacky and organized by people with bad taste
jokes.punchline: Then immediately admits he's throwing one
jokes.explanation: Perfect David contradiction - his snobbery immediately undermined by his love for Patrick. Well-structured self-aware hypocrisy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Surprise parties are tacky and organized by people with bad taste.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm throwing one.

jokes.text: When I planned that Casablanca-themed party for your mother's 40th, I had to quarantine the camels for a month.
jokes.setup: Johnny mentions planning an elaborate party
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of quarantining camels as if it's normal party planning
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd detail that reveals the family's former wealth and Johnny's earnest over-planning. The casual delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We'll need to quarantine the camels for at least two weeks before the party.

jokes.text: I've actually been organizing this for quite some time, I'm just telling you right now because I know that none of you can keep a secret.
jokes.setup: Family questions David's last-minute planning
jokes.punchline: David reveals he's been planning but doesn't trust them with secrets
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing David's control issues and family dysfunction, but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have been planning. I just don't trust any of you with secrets. Moira once told me she was keeping a surprise party for me, and then showed up to my birthday in a mermaid costume.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was a themed party.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My birthday isn't under the sea.

jokes.text: Oh! At 9 a.m. it's a little early for character assassination.
jokes.setup: David calls them bad at keeping secrets
jokes.punchline: Moira's dramatic overreaction calling it 'character assassination'
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira melodrama - turning mild criticism into dramatic persecution. Good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're all terrible at keeping secrets.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Character assassination! This is character assassination!

jokes.text: Not one of you knows how the 'Crows' movie ends.
jokes.setup: David claims they can't keep secrets
jokes.punchline: Evidence: they revealed the ending of 'The Crows' movie
jokes.explanation: Sets up the running gag about the horror movie, but fairly standard family bickering comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We can't keep secrets.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Evidence: they revealed the ending of 'The Crows' movie.

jokes.text: You told me the day you got home. / You gave me the script.
jokes.setup: David accuses them of spoiling the movie
jokes.punchline: Rapid-fire reveals that David told Johnny immediately and gave Alexis the script
jokes.explanation: Good escalation revealing David's hypocrisy - he's the one who can't keep secrets. Quick pacing works well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You spoiled the movie for me!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, I didn't tell anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, we would never do that.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh really? Because I remember you telling me about it the second you got home, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And you, Alexis—I literally gave you the script to read.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I thought that was confidential!

jokes.text: And you were both sworn to secrecy, so David is right not to trust you.
jokes.setup: Family reveals David told them movie secrets
jokes.punchline: Moira defends David while simultaneously proving his point wrong
jokes.explanation: Moira's misguided loyalty creates unintentional irony. Decent character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David would never betray a confidence. He's very good at keeping secrets. In fact, I didn't even know he was in a movie until he told me about it last week.

jokes.text: I invited Patrick's parents.
jokes.setup: David mentions an extra surprise
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he's invited the parents drops like a bomb
jokes.explanation: Effective dramatic beat that sets up the episode's central conflict. Great economy in the delivery.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have one more surprise for you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've invited your parents.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're here. They're waiting outside.

jokes.text: Yes, things are certainly racing along at a dangerously rapid velocity.
jokes.setup: Johnny says meeting parents is a big step
jokes.punchline: Moira's overwrought way of saying 'things are moving fast'
jokes.explanation: Pure Moira vocabulary excess - using unnecessarily dramatic language. Very character-specific humor.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, one moment you're meeting their parents, and the next moment you're buying a timeshare in Scottsdale and arguing about whose turn it is to use the bidet.

jokes.text: So tonight there will be a surprise. And there will be tears. Even if they're just my own. Okay?
jokes.setup: David promises this will be special
jokes.punchline: Escalates from surprise to tears, ending with his own emotional vulnerability
jokes.explanation: David's dramatic escalation combined with vulnerable admission. Good mix of comedy and character development.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This will be special.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've planned something you're not expecting.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I hope you're ready to be surprised.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm not sure I'm ready for this.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is harder than I thought it would be.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know why I'm getting emotional about this.

jokes.text: How did it end again? Like, a crowmageddon? / Crowpocalypse. But you didn't hear it from me.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks about the horror movie ending
jokes.punchline: Johnny gives increasingly ridiculous portmanteau titles, then pretends secrecy
jokes.explanation: Great callback with escalating silly movie titles. 'Crowpocalypse' is genuinely funny wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so what happens at the end?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I don't want to spoil it for you, but let's just say it's a real... Freddy Krueger meets Jaws situation. A Fredaws, if you will.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's more of a Nightmare on Elm Street meets The Ring type thing. A Nightmare Ring. Or a Ringtmare, really.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But I've already said too much. The studios really don't want me talking about this.

jokes.text: I mean, meeting my best friend's son's boyfriend's parents for the very first time, I am very touched that you wanted me to share this moment with you.
jokes.setup: Roland seems touched by the meaningful moment
jokes.punchline: The overly convoluted relationship description deflates the sentiment
jokes.explanation: Roland's characteristic over-explanation and self-insertion into meaningful moments. Length slightly hurts economy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, I'm really touched by this moment. You all mean so much to me.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You're like... well, you're my former business associates who are now my neighbors, but also kind of my friends, except we've had that thing where we weren't speaking for a bit, but now we're past that, so I guess you're more like... acquaintances who've become closer through proximity and shared experiences?

jokes.text: I asked you to mow the lawn three times, Roland, So you being here is more your decision than mine.
jokes.setup: Roland acts like Johnny invited him for the special moment
jokes.punchline: Johnny reveals Roland is just avoiding chores
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Roland's manufactured sentiment. Johnny's dry delivery works well.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This is nice, Johnny. I appreciate you thinking of me for this special moment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, you're not here because I invited you. You're here because Jazzagals is doing their spring cleaning today.

jokes.text: Oh, no, no! I do get that all the time, though. It's uh, you know, you've got David's handsome looks there, and then my face.
jokes.setup: Patrick's parents mistake Roland for David's father
jokes.punchline: Roland's backwards self-deprecating explanation of the resemblance
jokes.explanation: Roland's characteristic inability to take a compliment gracefully. Mildly amusing self-own.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, we're both very handsome men. It's an easy mistake to make.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: David's just got better bones, better skin, better hair, better everything really. But other than that, we're practically twins.

jokes.text: More like, runs it into the ground.
jokes.setup: Patrick's father mentions Johnny runs the motel
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately undermines Johnny in front of guests
jokes.explanation: Perfect Roland move - inappropriate honesty at exactly the wrong moment. Creates genuine cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, he runs it into the ground, if that's what you mean.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've seen better management at a lemonade stand.

jokes.text: Oh, we do have a lot of fun around here, don't we? / We do, sometimes.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to recover from Roland's insult with forced cheerfulness
jokes.punchline: Roland's grudging, qualified agreement
jokes.explanation: Good awkward beat. Roland's qualified enthusiasm undermines Johnny's attempt at damage control.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think that's great, Roland. I really do. And I'm sure that once people get to know me, they'll see past all of that and realize what a tremendous asset I could be to this community.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, well... you're not *completely* useless, I guess.

jokes.text: But uh, I'll have to admit, I've been somewhat of a skeptic about those two. / This one isn't as progressive as the rest of us. It's a generational thing with him.
jokes.setup: Johnny admits skepticism about David and Patrick
jokes.punchline: Roland assumes it's about them being gay, Johnny meant the business
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection creating maximum awkwardness. Roland's assumption reveals his own biases while creating misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I just have some concerns about the two of them going into business together.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I get it. Two guys, it's not for everybody.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, that's not what I meant. I'm talking about the business venture.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Right, right. Two guys in business together. I hear you.

jokes.text: I was talking about the store, Roland. You know, mixing a business relationship and a romantic relationship, well, that's... it can get kinda tricky sometimes.
jokes.setup: Roland labels Johnny as not progressive
jokes.punchline: Johnny clarifies he meant mixing business and romance, creating awkward silence
jokes.explanation: Payoff to the misdirection, but Johnny's explanation makes things more awkward. Good cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You're not very progressive, are you?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm very progressive. I just think mixing business and romance is a recipe for disaster.

jokes.text: Really? Because it kind of seems like the two of you just found out some really shocking information.
jokes.setup: The parents claim they knew Patrick and David were business partners
jokes.punchline: Roland bluntly calls out their obvious shock
jokes.explanation: Classic Roland - completely missing social cues and making everything worse. Character-perfect inappropriate honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you two looked real surprised when you found out. I mean, your jaws were on the ground. You didn't know they were business partners.

jokes.text: Huh. Correct me if I'm wrong here, Johnny. But it kinda seems like they didn't know their son is in a relationship with David.
jokes.setup: The entire awkward scene with the parents
jokes.punchline: Roland's masterful understatement of the obvious disaster
jokes.explanation: Roland's oblivious understatement perfectly caps the awkward scene. 'Kinda seems like' is perfect minimization.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, that didn't go great.

jokes.text: You're looking especially fresh and photogenic today, Mother.
jokes.setup: Alexis gives Moira an unusually specific compliment
jokes.punchline: The oddly professional language hints at ulterior motives
jokes.explanation: Sets up Alexis's scheme with suspiciously professional flattery. Good character setup.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, that color is really bringing out the sergeantatarms energy in your face.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Thank you, darling.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, so I was thinking, could you sign a permission slip for me?

jokes.text: there's a darling little medical clinic in Elm Grove. Where if any one of us, say, you, or your father, participates in a few nugatory tests, we could be bringing in over $200 a week!
jokes.setup: Moira announces a promising revenue stream
jokes.punchline: It's just medical testing, deflating the excitement
jokes.explanation: Moira's vocabulary ('nugatory') and desperate money schemes. 'Darling little medical clinic' is good characterization.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have some exciting news. I've found a promising new revenue stream for our family.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to be a test subject for medical trials.

jokes.text: Or, you could make $2,000 in one afternoon.
jokes.setup: Moira offers medical testing for $200/week
jokes.punchline: Sudden escalation to $2,000 reveals the real scheme
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and misdirection. The pause before revealing the real money grab works well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm offering comprehensive medical testing for only $200 a week.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, that's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if you'd prefer the premium package with bloodwork and imaging, that's $2,000 a week.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're running a medical scam out of our house?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I prefer to think of it as a wellness opportunity.

jokes.text: motley crew of pale, dewy-faced salamander people
jokes.setup: Moira describes convention attendees
jokes.punchline: Incredibly specific and elaborate insult comparing fans to salamanders
jokes.explanation: Peak Moira vocabulary - 'dewy-faced salamander people' is brilliantly specific and cruel. Highly quotable character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: They're like salamanders. Cold-blooded, slimy, and they reproduce by flinging their genetic material at each other indiscriminately.

jokes.text: You did a commercial for adult diapers! / In Japan! In full-face Kabuki makeup!
jokes.setup: Moira claims standards about self-worth
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals embarrassing commercial, Moira's increasingly desperate justifications
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with Moira's increasingly ridiculous justifications. Adult diaper commercial in Kabuki makeup is wonderfully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have certain standards about my self-worth.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, you did a commercial for hemorrhoid cream.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was a very tasteful commercial.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You were sitting on a bidet.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A French bidet. It was an art film.

jokes.text: But there will be a safe word in case the gaggle of asymmetrical faces gets to be too much.
jokes.setup: Moira agrees to do the convention
jokes.punchline: Needs a safe word for the 'gaggle of asymmetrical faces'
jokes.explanation: Another brilliant Moira insult - 'gaggle of asymmetrical faces' is poetry. The safe word concept adds absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I need a safe word for this gaggle of asymmetrical faces.

jokes.text: I told Patrick that there's a salmonella outbreak at the cafe, so he will not be coming here until I tell him to tonight.
jokes.setup: David needs to keep Patrick away from the party setup
jokes.punchline: His unnecessarily elaborate and potentially damaging lie
jokes.explanation: David's characteristic overthinking - choosing the worst possible lie. Good character comedy about his anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I need you to stay away from the store tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Why? What's going on?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, the building is being treated for a very rare and aggressive form of mold. It's called... Fusarium Oxysporum Roseum. It's highly contagious and can cause severe respiratory damage, hair loss, and in some cases, temporary blindness.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The health inspector called this morning. We're completely quarantined until Thursday. You absolutely cannot come in. In fact, no one can. It's a biohazard situation.

jokes.text: Oh. Is it possible you could maybe... choose a lie that doesn't make the cafe look like it's in violation of health codes?
jokes.setup: David announces his salmonella outbreak lie
jokes.punchline: Stevie's dry observation about the business implications
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie deadpan - practical concern delivered with characteristic dryness. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So you lied about a salmonella outbreak to get out of a business meeting, and now the Rose Apothecary is closed, and we're hemorrhaging money.

jokes.text: Did you run here, Mr. Rose? / No! Just a pleasant walk.
jokes.setup: Johnny arrives at the cafe
jokes.punchline: Stevie's observation suggests he's clearly out of breath from rushing
jokes.explanation: Visual gag inferred from dialogue - Johnny's obvious disheveled state contrasted with his denial.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie, I made it. I'm here.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I can see that. You're sweating through your shirt and breathing like you just ran a marathon.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was in a hurry.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: To get here? Or to leave?

jokes.text: What? They're not supposed to be here for another 11 minutes!
jokes.setup: Johnny says he checked in the parents
jokes.punchline: David's oddly specific timing obsession
jokes.explanation: Perfect David anxiety - the specificity of '11 minutes' reveals his control obsession. Very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you tell them about the blackout curtains? Because they need to be drawn by 6:47 PM. Not 6:45, not 6:50. 6:47.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because that's when the sun hits the west-facing window at the precise angle that causes a glare on the television screen, and I've timed it.

jokes.text: at one point Patrick was engaged ahem... to a woman, so...
jokes.setup: Johnny asks if the parents know David and Patrick are dating
jokes.punchline: The awkward trailing off after mentioning Patrick's female ex-fiancée
jokes.explanation: Good awkward beat. Johnny's discomfort with the topic creates natural comedy through hesitation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Do your mother and I know that you and Patrick are dating?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And you're both... comfortable with that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Dad. Very much so.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's... that's wonderful. I mean, he seems like a very nice man. Very successful. And his ex-fiancée, Stevie, she's a lovely...

jokes.text: romantically in business with each other
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to explain the parents' reaction to David and Patrick's relationship
jokes.punchline: The awkward phrase 'romantically in business' reveals his discomfort
jokes.explanation: Johnny's awkward word choice creates unintentional humor. The phrase sounds accidentally dirty.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, your mother and I, we're very supportive of you and Patrick, but we're just trying to understand the dynamic here. You're business partners and you're also... romantically in business together.

jokes.text: Ew!
jokes.setup: Johnny says they're 'romantically in business'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's disgusted reaction to the phrase
jokes.explanation: Perfect reaction beat. Maximum economy - one word that perfectly captures everyone's reaction to Johnny's phrasing.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Romantically in business? That's not a thing.

jokes.text: I wouldn't have asked them to come here if I didn't think they knew we were in a relationship! They call the store all the time! I mean, what do they think, I'm just his business partner?!
jokes.setup: David insists the parents must know about the relationship
jokes.punchline: His escalating panic and the ironic final question
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing David's panic. The irony is that yes, they did think he was just a business partner.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They have to know. They have to know about us.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, what if they find out from someone else? What if they hear it on the street?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We live in a town of 5,000 people. Someone's going to tell them.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And then what? Then I'm a liar. I'm someone who hid this from his own parents.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you understand what that makes me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A bad son. A secretive, deceptive bad son.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So, are you... are you ashamed of me?

jokes.text: Possibly. / I mean, yeah.
jokes.setup: David asks rhetorically if the parents think he's just a business partner
jokes.punchline: Johnny and Stevie's matter-of-fact agreement
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of David's rhetorical question. The casual agreement is brutally honest and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, yes.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: Everybody calm down! Nobody say a word to anybody! / Well, that sounds reasonable. / What did I just say?!
jokes.setup: David demands everyone stay calm and silent
jokes.punchline: Johnny immediately responds, violating the silence rule
jokes.explanation: Classic immediate violation of the rule just established. Johnny's obliviousness creates perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Everyone needs to stay calm and silent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: There is a dark side to Mummy's industry, and I've tried to protect you from it your whole life. / You took me to the Playboy Mansion when I was seven.
jokes.setup: Moira claims she's protected Alexis from industry darkness
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately points out the Playboy Mansion visit at age 7
jokes.explanation: Excellent contradiction revealing the family's warped normal. The specific age (7) makes it funnier and more shocking.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have protected you from the darkness of this industry.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, you took me to the Playboy Mansion when I was seven.

jokes.text: And you had a wonderful time in the children's grotto.
jokes.setup: Alexis mentions being taken to the Playboy Mansion at 7
jokes.punchline: Moira's casual mention of a 'children's grotto' at the Playboy Mansion
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant absurdist detail. The idea of a children's area at the Playboy Mansion is wonderfully ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, there's a children's grotto.

jokes.text: Who's Gloria Gregson?! She only played Grace on 'Touched With Grace' for 37 years. The woman has 17 Emmys for playing a hot-headed nurse who healed people with her sexual touch.
jokes.setup: Alexis doesn't know who Gloria Gregson is
jokes.punchline: Moira's elaborate description of the ridiculous soap opera character
jokes.explanation: Wonderful absurd soap opera parody. 'Healed people with her sexual touch' is perfect ridiculous soap logic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Gloria Gregson is only the most iconic character in daytime television history. She's a former trapeze artist turned neurologist who discovered a cure for a fictional disease, had an affair with her own son—not biologically, it was very complicated—faked her own death twice, came back as her evil twin, and once performed an emergency C-section on herself using only a letter opener and her wits.

jokes.text: Oh! Daughter? More like sister. Ew.
jokes.setup: Alexis introduces herself as Moira's daughter
jokes.punchline: Gloria's inappropriate comment followed by her own 'Ew'
jokes.explanation: Awkward older actress behavior. Gloria's immediate 'Ew' at her own comment adds self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: Oh honey, with a face like that, I'm surprised your mother didn't leave you at the hospital.

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: Ew.

jokes.text: Um...
jokes.setup: David asks if Patrick's parents know about 'us'
jokes.punchline: Patrick's long, uncomfortable pause revealing the truth
jokes.explanation: Powerful silent beat. Sometimes the absence of words is the joke - Patrick's hesitation says everything.

jokes.text: there is a small chance that this could change everything. That they might see me differently, or treat me differently.
jokes.setup: Patrick explains why he hasn't told his parents
jokes.punchline: His vulnerable admission of fear creates uncomfortable honesty
jokes.explanation: More emotional than comedic, but the vulnerability creates character-based humor through the awkward honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I haven't told them because I'm terrified. I'm terrified they won't accept me, I'm terrified they'll reject me, and I'm terrified that everything I've built with David will somehow be less than because of who I am.

jokes.text: That's why I brought this couple home one day in college, and just told my parents to deal with it.
jokes.setup: David offers support about coming out to parents
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation about bringing home 'a couple' (threesome implication)
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - starts supportive, ends with casual revelation of sexual adventurousness. Very David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, when I came out to my parents, I was terrified. But they were actually very supportive. So whenever you're ready to tell them about... whoever it is you're seeing, I'm sure they'll be fine with it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Although, I should mention—when I say 'supportive,' I mean they didn't disown me. If you bring home a couple, that might test their limits a bit.

jokes.text: You're gonna laugh yourself right out of the closet on this one.
jokes.setup: David prepares to tell Patrick about the surprise party
jokes.punchline: Plays with the 'out of the closet' phrase in context of coming out story
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay given the context. David's comfort with gay terminology used in unexpected way.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I need to tell you something. I've been keeping this secret, and I just came out of the closet.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Literally. I was hiding in the closet to surprise you for your party.

jokes.text: I could be just your business partner tonight. If that will help.
jokes.setup: David realizes his mistake with inviting the parents
jokes.punchline: Offers to go back in the closet for Patrick's comfort
jokes.explanation: Sweet and funny offer showing David's growth. The casualness of offering to hide their relationship is both sad and funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can go back in the closet if that would make you more comfortable.

jokes.text: The salmonella was a decoy.
jokes.setup: Patrick asks if the party is at the cafe
jokes.punchline: David's spy-movie language for his food poisoning lie
jokes.explanation: Good callback with dramatic spy language. 'Decoy' makes the lie sound more elaborate than it was.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Is the party at the cafe?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I have a prior commitment. I've been... compromised.

jokes.text: So you're not in a relationship with our son? / Oh, no. I very much am.
jokes.setup: David apologizes for miscommunication, mother asks for clarification
jokes.punchline: David's matter-of-fact confirmation creates more confusion
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - David's casual confirmation when they're hoping for denial. Creates more awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I apologize for the miscommunication.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, what exactly did you mean to communicate?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What I said.

jokes.text: David, we're not upset about Patrick being gay. / No. / Oh my God, okay! For a minute I thought this was gonna get very dark.
jokes.setup: David assumes they're homophobic
jokes.punchline: Parents clarify they're not upset about the gay part, David's relief
jokes.explanation: Good relief moment. David's 'very dark' comment acknowledges the real-world stakes while providing comic relief.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're not... upset? About the gay part?

dialogue.speaker: Marci
dialogue.line: Upset? No, honey. We love you.

dialogue.speaker: Clint
dialogue.line: It's everything else about you we can't stand.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, thank God.

jokes.text: keep him in the closet until then. I know that came out wrong, but we all understand what I'm saying, right?
jokes.setup: David suggests pretending they don't know about the relationship
jokes.punchline: Realizes his unfortunate word choice and acknowledges it
jokes.explanation: Self-aware comedy acknowledging the unfortunate phrasing. David's awareness makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We could just pretend we don't know about it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: ...I see how that sounded.

jokes.text: 'Touched With Grapes.' / Oh my God, that's your wine? / Yes. We used to use that for sangria, because it was like, so cheap, and... so good.
jokes.setup: Gloria mentions her wine brand
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals they used it for cheap sangria, Gloria's defensive response
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Gloria's pretension. The wine name 'Touched With Grapes' is absurd, and Alexis's honesty stings.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We actually used your wine for sangria.

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: Sangria?! That's a crime against my brand!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it was cheap sangria...

jokes.text: $20?! I charge $50. And just in case, I wear open-toed sandals.
jokes.setup: Moira asks about exploiting extremities for $20
jokes.punchline: Gloria's higher price and the preparation detail about sandals
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about convention culture with specific business details. The sandal preparation is wonderfully mercenary.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you won't exploit your extremities for twenty dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: Twenty dollars? I charge fifty. And I have to prepare my feet. I can't just show up in sandals.

jokes.text: And if we're talking feet, it's $75 for one, $150 for the pair.
jokes.setup: Alexis takes over managing Moira's convention appearance
jokes.punchline: Immediately establishes fee structure for feet photos
jokes.explanation: Alexis's business acumen applied to bizarre situation. The specific pricing structure is professionally absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so I'm thinking we charge $50 for a standard feet photo, $75 if you want her to hold something, and $100 if you want her to do like a pose or a thing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, why would anyone be paying for photographs of my feet?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know, but apparently there's a market for it. We'd be leaving money on the table if we didn't capitalize on it.

jokes.text: Also, we're going with 'Surprise.' 'Happy Birthday' was too wordy, and we couldn't nail it at rehearsal.
jokes.setup: Organizing the surprise party timing
jokes.punchline: Reducing the chant to one word due to coordination issues
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie practicality - treating party coordination like stage management. 'Too wordy' for 'Happy Birthday' is great.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, so we need to coordinate the surprise. Everyone chant 'Surprise!' on three.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's not going to work. Half of you can't count to three.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I can count to three!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Last time we tried this, someone yelled it on two.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fine. New plan. We just all yell 'Surprise' together.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're still going to mess it up. We need something simpler.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: One word. Just yell 'Hey!' Everyone can handle one word.

jokes.text: But might I recommend the crab cakes? I over-ordered, and they cost a small fortune.
jokes.setup: David making polite party host conversation
jokes.punchline: Immediately brings up the financial mistake he made
jokes.explanation: Perfect David anxiety - can't make a recommendation without mentioning the cost stress. Very characteristic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So, how are you enjoying the party?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: By the way, I've been meaning to tell you about this absolutely catastrophic real estate investment I made in 2008.

jokes.text: I like him. I like him a lot. / Me too. / I don't understand his clothes, but...
jokes.setup: Patrick's parents accepting David
jokes.punchline: Father's honest confusion about David's fashion sense
jokes.explanation: Sweet moment with gentle humor. The father's fashion confusion is relatable and non-judgmental.

dialogue.speaker: Clint
dialogue.line: So David, your fashion sense is very... unique.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Clint
dialogue.line: No, I mean — I genuinely don't understand what I'm looking at.

jokes.text: Ah, you will in time.
jokes.setup: Patrick's father doesn't understand David's clothes
jokes.punchline: David's confident prediction about fashion conversion
jokes.explanation: David's supreme confidence in his fashion influence. Good character moment showing his self-assurance.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He'll come around. Everyone does. It's only a matter of time before he realizes that I'm right about fashion, and he's been dressing like a cast member from Cops.

jokes.text: Well, I guess that makes up for the fact that no one ate the crab cakes.
jokes.setup: Patrick says it was one of the happiest nights
jokes.punchline: David immediately brings up the food waste issue again
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing David can't let go of the financial anxiety even in a sweet moment. Very characteristic.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It was one of the happiest nights.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm glad you felt that way, but we really do need to talk about the food waste issue.

jokes.text: David, I know my parents. They're not good actors. They knew about us, right?
jokes.setup: Patrick realizes his parents weren't surprised enough
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact assessment of their acting ability
jokes.explanation: Good character knowledge - Patrick understanding his parents' limitations. Shows his emotional intelligence.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: My parents weren't surprised enough when I told them I was getting married. They were just like, 'Oh, that's nice.' So I've come to the conclusion that my parents are just bad actors.

jokes.text: And the gift basket that you gave them that I'm hoping you paid for, that had nothing to do with you trying to smooth things over with them?
jokes.setup: Patrick putting together David's behavior
jokes.punchline: The specific detail about hoping David paid for the gift basket
jokes.explanation: Patrick's knowledge of David's patterns, including his tendency to be generous with store inventory rather than his own money.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm just putting together that David's behavior... I'm hoping he at least paid for the gift basket.

tmdb_overview: Patrick’s parents are in town for his surprise party, but the parents end up with an even bigger surprise, while Alexis drags Moira to a soap opera convention.
insight: Family awkwardness and David's restrained panic drive a solid but uneven character-comedy episode.
